A sweet song drifts from a cloud shelf where nothing should be standing. The harpy waits for one distracted step before diving through the mist.
A giant eagle stoops from the high blue with a warning scream. Its nest is hidden nearby, and it will drive intruders away from the thermal.
Tiny shapes tumble inside a dirty hailcloud, laughing as ice rattles around them. They spill out with claws and breath like winter spite.
Warm air rises in invisible steps along a cliff or cloudbank. Reading the lift correctly turns a dangerous climb or flight into an easy glide.
The air drops away without warning, taking loose gear and stomachs with it. Fast reactions keep the fall from becoming a tumble.
A bright ring circles the sun through high ice crystals. The whole sky seems briefly marked by a silent omen.
Cloudberries cling to a windswept ledge just below the cloud line. The fruit bruises easily, and the ledge offers little room for knees.
Pale lichen grows on stones scoured by constant wind. Scraping it loose without losing it to the gusts takes careful hands.
A raptor's abandoned nest is woven with fragrant herbs that repel parasites. The useful sprigs can be gathered if the nest is truly empty.
A rain-fed pool on a high shelf holds tiny silver minnows stranded after a storm. They vanish beneath stone at the first shadow.
Trout gather in a plunge pool below a high waterfall where spray turns the air white. Casting through the mist is the hard part.
A ribbon-like eel glides through a floating pocket of stormwater. It snaps at hooks and vanishes when thunder rolls.
A lightning strike has fused sand and grit into branching glass on a high ridge. The fragile pieces must be lifted whole.
Porous pale stones lie where a floating rock shed its outer crust. They are light but crumble under careless grip.
A red ridge shows iron where wind has stripped the soil clean. Mining it means anchoring tools against sudden gusts.
A lone high pine has split cleanly under lightning, leaving dry lengths raised above the rain. Cutting them loose is a balancing act.
An abandoned aerie holds bleached branches and old nest beams carried from far below. The larger pieces are tangled into the stone.
A spruce has fallen just below the ridge where wind keeps the ground dry. Its straight trunk can be cut before weather rolls in.
A shadow crosses the sun twice before an aarakocra dives with spear leveled. The warning cry names a forbidden thermal in Auran.
A giant hawk stoops from a cloudbank wearing a silver jess large as a belt. It treats the party as stray quarry from its master's preserve.
A clear patch of sky twists into a roaring funnel that hates wings, sails, and loose rope. The shear cuts before it can be seen.
Old sky-sailors mark invisible roads by watching cloud shadows race over peaks. Reading the current turns a punishing climb into a swift passage.
Hair lifts and metal hums as a thunderhead rolls under the party. Dropping low or shedding charge keeps lightning from choosing a target.
A bright halo around the moon makes the clouds look like silver islands. For a moment, every high road seems visible at once.
White cloudcap fungus grows where mist clings to a cliff face. It must be cut during cloud cover before sunlight dries it flat.
Thunder sage grows in cracks struck by lightning. Its leaves prickle with stored charge and should be wrapped in cloth.
Birds have woven berry canes through an abandoned eyrie. The fruit is clean, tart, and awkward to reach.
A storm has filled a natural stone barrel high on a ridge, trapping tiny trout swept from a stream. The water will drain by morning.
Clear shrimp drift in a mist-fed pool beneath a waterfall ledge. They vanish against the stone when shadows pass.
A small cloud ray fry glides through a pocket of dense fog. Catching it safely takes gentleness more than strength.
A storm opal chip glows in glass left by a lightning strike. It is easy to miss until thunder mutters overhead.
Floating pumice pebbles rain from a broken cloudstone. They bob away from heavy steps and cling to damp cloth.
A needle of sky-iron juts from a wind-scoured ridge where a falling star struck. Pulling it free takes leverage against fierce gusts.
A cedar snag on a high ledge has dried hard and aromatic in constant wind. The straight sections are worth the climb.
Crate slats from a skyship spill lie caught in thorn bushes below a cliff. Many are lightwood and still stamped with port marks.
An old aerie beam is pegged into the cliff by giant iron spikes. Freeing it safely means removing the pegs in order.
